mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz CLK 200 CDI or 220 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM611 or OM646 engine typically costs £2,000–£5,000 supply only depending on which generation. Used units are available from £1,500–£3,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your CLK.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz CLK 200 CDI / 220 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM611 and OM646 are robust and economical diesels, but they have well-documented weaknesses. Injector seal failure ('Black Death') is the most common issue on both engines. Later OM646 engines can suffer from DPF and EGR problems. With good maintenance, these engines are capable of high mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz CLK 200 CDI and 220 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector seal failure ('Black Death' — tar-like sludge around injectors), DPF and EGR clogging (OM646), inlet manifold flap problems (OM611), timing chain stretch, and oil dilution from failed DPF regenerations. The injector seals are the most critical concern.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz CLK 200/220 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: CLK values range from £2,000–£10,000 depending on condition and spec. A £2,000–£5,000 reconditioned engine is 20–50% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz CLK 200 CDI or 220 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. C208/A208 (1998–2002) uses OM611.962; C209/A209 (2002–2009) uses OM646.963 (200 CDI) or OM646.963/.966 (220 CDI). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 'Black Death' injector seals, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The injector seals can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £300–£800 and requires specialist tools to re-cut the injector seats. If the injector has seized in the head, extraction can be difficult and expensive. A preventative seal replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. At the first sign of ticking or black sludge around the injectors, book an inspection immediately.

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz CLK 200 CDI / 220 CDI Engine Replacement — Compare Prices & Get Quotes
hero.subHeadline: The CLK 200 CDI and 220 CDI are powered by Mercedes' first-generation common-rail diesels — the OM611 in earlier cars and the improved OM646 in later models. They're smooth, economical and torquey, but injector seal failures (Black Death), EGR/DPF issues and inlet flap problems are well-documented. Get quotes from UK specialists for reconditioned, rebuilt and used units today.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz CLK 200/220 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current CLK CDI market. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. OM646 engines command a slight premium over OM611 units. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLK CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The longitudinal layout makes access relatively straightforward.

cta.label: → Compare CLK CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Injector Seal Failure ("Black Death") — OM611 & OM646
problems.symptoms: Ticking or chuffing noise from the top of the engine, diesel smell inside or around the car, rough idle, loss of power, hard starting, thick black tar-like sludge around the injectors
problems.replacementCost: £2,000–£4,500 (if injector seizure damages the cylinder head)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: injector seal failure is the defining issue on both OM611 and OM646 engines

problems.recommendation: Mercedes CDI engines are notoriously susceptible to "Black Death" — a condition where injector seals fail, allowing combustion gases to escape past the injectors. The escaping gases create a thick, tar-like sludge around the affected injectors. If left unchecked, the injector can seize in the head, requiring expensive extraction work. Lift the engine cover and inspect the area around each injector for black sludge — any sign of leakage requires immediate attention. Replacement of the seals and re-cutting of the injector seats is a specialist job.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector seal failure guide

problems.title: DPF and EGR System Problems — OM646 (2004+)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, limp mode, poor fuel economy, failed regenerations, oil dilution (rising oil level)
problems.recommendation: Later OM646 engines fitted with DPFs and EGR systems are prone to clogging, particularly if the car is used for short journeys. Failed DPF regenerations can cause oil dilution and rising oil levels. If the DPF warning light appears, take the car for a sustained motorway run to allow regeneration. If the issue persists, have the DPF and EGR system inspected. Regular motorway driving is essential for these engines.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF and EGR failure guide

problems.title: Inlet Manifold Flap Problems — OM611 (Early)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, engine warning light, poor fuel economy, hesitation on acceleration
problems.recommendation: Early OM611 engines can suffer from inlet manifold flap problems. The flaps can become stuck or break, causing loss of power and poor running. Replacement of the inlet manifold is the usual fix. Some owners choose to delete the flaps entirely, though this may affect emissions compliance. If you experience hesitation or loss of power, have the inlet manifold inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full inlet manifold failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: CLK 200/220 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch early; seat re-cutting may be required
Viable on a cherished example; borderline on high-mileage
Forced regeneration or cleaning — prevention is key
Labour-intensive but cheaper than a full engine

buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLK 200/220 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2004 OM646 with documented DPF and injector service history
* OM611 with evidence of injector seal work — the defining weakness addressed
* Full service history with correct MB-spec oil
* Evidence of regular motorway driving (essential for DPF health)
avoidIf: * Any ticking or chuffing from the top of the engine — injector seal "Black Death" is likely
* Black tar-like sludge around the injectors — injector seal failure is imminent
* DPF warning light or limp mode — regeneration issues are expensive to fix
* Poor service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLK 200 CDI and 220 CDI are powered by Mercedes' first-generation common-rail diesels — smooth, economical and torquey. The OM611 (1998–2002) is a robust engine with injector seal and inlet flap issues. The OM646 (2002–2009) is the refined successor with improved injection pressure, but DPF and EGR problems on later cars. Both suffer from the notorious "Black Death" injector seal failure — inspect the injector area carefully before buying. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the CLK CDI will reward you with effortless cruising.

cta.label: → Compare CLK CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz CLK 200 CDI or 220 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Injector Area — Lift the engine cover and check for black tar-like sludge around the injectors ("Black Death" — the most critical check on both OM611 and OM646)
2. Cold Start — Any ticking or chuffing from the top of the engine? (injector seal warning)
3. Service History — including which generation (OM611 or OM646) and evidence of injector work
4. DPF Health (OM646) — Any warning lights or reduced power? (DPF clogging is common)
5. Oil Level — Is the oil level rising? (oil dilution from failed DPF regenerations)
6. Performance — Any hesitation or loss of power? (inlet flap or EGR issues)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, NOx sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — two engine families across the CLK CDI's history.
